unit.name: DPO versus PPO, online preference optimization, and future directions
unit.description: Compares DPO and PPO empirically, explains online data, introduces D2PO-style methods, and interprets industry post-training recipes.
unit.summary: Lambert moves from reward-model evaluation to whether the final aligned policy improves, describing empirical DPO-versus-PPO work starting from an instruction-tuned Llama 2 13B [ts=2390s]. Instruction tuning gives the largest jump on reasoning, coding, and chat tasks; DPO with Anthropic Helpful-Harmless gives a small bump, while switching only to UltraFeedback gives a larger 0–2% style gain, showing how important data is [ts=2424s]. PPO reliably gives about 1% better average results than DPO in the shown 13B experiments, but bigger reward models and added code/reasoning prompts do not cleanly improve the full average, and PPO has many knobs such as regularization, value-function learning, warmup, and size parameters [ts=2501s]. Its major bottleneck is online generation of fresh responses during training, making it much slower than DPO, so the extra signal may or may not be worth the effort for small teams [ts=2678s]. He then defines online alignment in terms of freshly generated data and refreshed chosen/rejected labels, contrasting UltraFeedback’s mixture of many models’ generations with PPO’s repeated sampling from the current policy [ts=2828s]. Recent papers are said to converge on the importance of online data, and newer DPO variants include self-rewarding LMs, batched relabeling, and D2PO, which compares standard DPO, reward-model relabeling, and reward-model retraining over time [ts=2941s]. The section connects this to industry: Llama 2-style pipelines collect new human data from previous checkpoints, and Meta’s Llama 3 recipe combining SFT, rejection sampling, PPO, and DPO is interpreted as a practical iterative process [ts=3211s]. Future directions include fresh data, more DPO variants, changing the reference model/loss, studying more model sizes including small models, better targeted evaluations, and personalization [ts=3372s].

key_points.text: Instruction tuning gives the largest capability jump before preference optimization.
UltraFeedback improves DPO more than noisy Anthropic Helpful-Harmless data in the shown recipe.
PPO gives roughly 1% better average results than DPO but is much harder and slower to tune.
PPO’s major bottleneck is generating new model responses during training to refresh data.
Online alignment separates fresh policy-generated text from refreshed chosen/rejected labels.
Self-rewarding language models and D2PO try to make DPO more online through relabeling and reward-model updates.
Industry post-training appears iterative, combining SFT, rejection sampling, DPO, PPO, and repeated human-data collection.
Future alignment work should target data, DPO variants, smaller scales, evaluation specificity, and personalization.

unit_summary_for_generation: Lambert moves from reward-model evaluation to whether the final aligned policy improves, describing empirical DPO-versus-PPO work starting from an instruction-tuned Llama 2 13B [ts=2390s]. Instruction tuning gives the largest jump on reasoning, coding, and chat tasks; DPO with Anthropic Helpful-Harmless gives a small bump, while switching only to UltraFeedback gives a larger 0–2% style gain, showing how important data is [ts=2424s]. PPO reliably gives about 1% better average results than DPO in the shown 13B experiments, but bigger reward models and added code/reasoning prompts do not cleanly improve the full average, and PPO has many knobs such as regularization, value-function learning, warmup, and size parameters [ts=2501s]. Its major bottleneck is online generation of fresh responses during training, making it much slower than DPO, so the extra signal may or may not be worth the effort for small teams [ts=2678s]. He then defines online alignment in terms of freshly generated data and refreshed chosen/rejected labels, contrasting UltraFeedback’s mixture of many models’ generations with PPO’s repeated sampling from the current policy [ts=2828s]. Recent papers are said to converge on the importance of online data, and newer DPO variants include self-rewarding LMs, batched relabeling, and D2PO, which compares standard DPO, reward-model relabeling, and reward-model retraining over time [ts=2941s]. The section connects this to industry: Llama 2-style pipelines collect new human data from previous checkpoints, and Meta’s Llama 3 recipe combining SFT, rejection sampling, PPO, and DPO is interpreted as a practical iterative process [ts=3211s]. Future directions include fresh data, more DPO variants, changing the reference model/loss, studying more model sizes including small models, better targeted evaluations, and personalization [ts=3372s].

kp_catalog.name: DPO versus PPO and online methods
kp_catalog.description: The empirical and conceptual tradeoff between simple DPO, more costly PPO, and online DPO variants.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dPO versus PPO and online methods formalizes how the empirical and conceptual tradeoff between simple DPO, more costly PPO, and online DPO variants, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Industry recipes and future directions
kp_catalog.description: Iterative SFT/rejection-sampling/DPO/PPO pipelines plus data, scale, evaluation, and personalization directions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because industry recipes and future directions clarifies iterative SFT/rejection-sampling/DPO/PPO pipelines plus data, scale, evaluation, and personalization directions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Open alignment data and adoption
kp_catalog.description: ShareGPT, Open Assistant, UltraFeedback, Zephyr, and Tulu 2 as drivers of open alignment progress.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because open alignment data and adoption clarifies shareGPT, Open Assistant, UltraFeedback, Zephyr, and Tulu 2 as drivers of open alignment progress, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
